subtopic_detail: DKA - initial fluid and insulin protocol
stem:
A 19-year-old man with type 1 diabetes mellitus presents to a HNE LHD emergency department with 24 hours of polyuria, abdominal pain and vomiting after a viral gastroenteritis. He is drowsy but rousable. Examination: dry mucous membranes, deep sighing respirations, capillary refill 4 seconds. Heart rate 122, blood pressure 96/58 mmHg, SpO2 99% room air, respiratory rate 28. Capillary blood glucose 28.4 mmol/L, capillary ketones 5.6 mmol/L.

Venous blood gas: pH 7.08, HCO3 7 mmol/L, base excess -22. Sodium 132, potassium 5.2, chloride 98, urea 9.2, creatinine 118 micromol/L. He weighs 70 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 122, BP 96/58, RR 28, SpO2 99% RA, GCS 14, weight 70 kg.
data_table.Electrolytes: Na 132, K 5.2, Cl 98, urea 9.2, creatinine 118 mcmol/L.
data_table.Hydration: Dry mucosae, CRT 4 s, BP 96/58, HR 122.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial fluid and insulin order?

options.text: Sodium chloride 0.9% 1000 mL over 60 minutes, then start insulin infusion 0.1 unit/kg/h
options.rationale: NSW Health adult DKA pathway: restore circulating volume first with 0.9% saline 1000 mL over the first hour, then commence fixed-rate insulin infusion at 0.1 unit/kg/h. Insulin started simultaneously with adequate volume resuscitation, not before, to avoid worsening hypotension as glucose-driven osmotic shift reverses.
source_refs: NSW Health Adult DKA management guideline
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Diabetic ketoacidosis

options.text: Sodium chloride 0.9% 1000 mL over 60 minutes plus bolus insulin 10 units intravenously
options.rationale: Bolus insulin is not recommended in adult DKA per NSW Health and JBDS-IP pathways. It risks rapid potassium shift and adds nothing over the fixed-rate infusion (NSW Health).
source_refs: NSW Health Adult DKA management guideline

options.text: Insulin infusion 0.1 unit/kg/h before commencing intravenous fluids
options.rationale: Starting insulin before fluid resuscitation in a shocked patient drives intracellular potassium shift on top of hypovolaemia, worsening haemodynamics and risking arrhythmia. Fluid first or simultaneously (NSW Health).
source_refs: NSW Health Adult DKA management guideline

options.text: Compound sodium lactate 1000 mL over 60 minutes plus insulin infusion
options.rationale: 0.9% saline is the standard initial fluid in DKA in NSW Health pathways. Hartmann's (compound sodium lactate) is sometimes used in select centres but contains lactate (converted to bicarbonate) and small potassium load; 0.9% saline remains the protocol default (NSW Health).
source_refs: NSW Health Adult DKA management guideline

options.text: Sodium bicarbonate 8.4% 100 mL over 30 minutes, then insulin and fluid
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is not routinely recommended in DKA even with pH <7.0. Risks include paradoxical CSF acidosis, hypokalaemia, and impaired tissue oxygen delivery. Reserved for severe acidosis (pH <6.9) with haemodynamic instability (NSW Health).
source_refs: NSW Health Adult DKA management guideline

explanation.summary: NSW Health DKA pathway: first hour is 1 L 0.9% saline plus fixed-rate insulin infusion 0.1 unit/kg/h. Add 5% glucose alongside saline when BGL falls below 14 mmol/L to allow continued insulin (which clears ketones) without causing hypoglycaemia. Potassium replacement starts in the second bag once K <5.5 mmol/L.
key_points: 0.9% saline 1 L hour 1, then titrate to deficit (typically 6-10 L deficit)
Fixed-rate insulin 0.1 unit/kg/h; do not bolus
Add 10% glucose when BGL <14 to maintain insulin while preventing hypoglycaemia
Continue subcutaneous long-acting insulin during DKA treatment if type 1 diabetic
explanation.context: DKA pathophysiology: absolute insulin deficiency drives lipolysis and hepatic ketogenesis (beta-hydroxybutyrate, acetoacetate), producing an anion gap metabolic acidosis. Hyperglycaemia causes osmotic diuresis with severe sodium, water and potassium losses. Total body potassium is depleted even when serum potassium appears normal or high (extracellular shift from acidosis and insulin deficiency). Insulin reverses ketogenesis (its primary therapeutic effect) and drives potassium intracellularly, hence the need for aggressive replacement once treatment starts.

sources.label: NSW Health Adult DKA management guideline
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Diabetic ketoacidosis

stem: A 24-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes mellitus is being managed for diabetic ketoacidosis. She has received 1 L of 0.9% saline over the first hour and insulin infusion at 7 units/h has been running for 60 minutes. Her second bag of 0.9% saline is being set up. Current results: pH 7.14, HCO3 9, BGL 22 mmol/L, sodium 138, potassium 4.6, urea 8.0, creatinine 92 micromol/L. Urine output 60 mL in the past hour.

data_table.Status: 1 h into DKA treatment, hour 2 fluid being set up.
data_table.Bloods now: pH 7.14, HCO3 9, BGL 22, Na 138, K 4.6, urea 8.0, creatinine 92.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate potassium addition to the next litre of saline?

options.text: No potassium added; recheck after this bag
options.rationale: Insulin is driving potassium intracellularly. Waiting until potassium drops below 3.5 risks symptomatic hypokalaemia (arrhythmia, weakness, paralytic ileus). Replacement begins when K <5.5 with adequate urine output (NSW Health).
source_refs: NSW Health Adult DKA management guideline

options.text: Add 40 mmol potassium chloride to the second-hour fluid
options.rationale: NSW Health DKA pathway: with serum potassium 3.5-5.5 mmol/L and adequate urine output, add 40 mmol KCl per litre. The patient's K is 4.6 (within this range) and urine output is adequate (>0.5 mL/kg/h), making this the standard step. Higher rates (60 mmol/L) require central access and continuous cardiac monitoring.
source_refs: NSW Health Adult DKA management guideline
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - DKA

options.text: Add 20 mmol potassium chloride to the second-hour fluid
options.rationale: 20 mmol/L is insufficient to keep up with the ongoing insulin-driven intracellular shift. NSW Health protocol mandates 40 mmol/L in this serum potassium range with adequate urine output (NSW Health).
source_refs: NSW Health Adult DKA management guideline

options.text: Stop insulin infusion until potassium rises to 5.0
options.rationale: Stopping insulin is only indicated if K falls below 3.5 mmol/L; without insulin, ketogenesis resumes and acidosis worsens. K of 4.6 is normal-range and falling; replace potassium, do not stop insulin (NSW Health).
source_refs: NSW Health Adult DKA management guideline

options.text: Give oral potassium 40 mmol slow-release tablet
options.rationale: Oral potassium is unreliable in a patient with vomiting and gastroparesis from ketoacidosis. Intravenous route gives predictable plasma levels and is the protocol standard (NSW Health).
source_refs: NSW Health Adult DKA management guideline

explanation.summary: NSW Health potassium replacement in DKA: K >5.5 add none, K 3.5-5.5 add 40 mmol/L, K <3.5 hold insulin and replace at 20 mmol/h via central line until K >3.5, then resume insulin with continued replacement. Adequate urine output (>0.5 mL/kg/h) is required before potassium is added.
key_points: K >5.5: hold replacement, recheck in 2 h
K 3.5-5.5: 40 mmol/L of fluid
K <3.5: stop insulin, replace at 20 mmol/h via CVC until K >3.5
Urine output >0.5 mL/kg/h required before adding potassium
explanation.context: DKA depletes total body potassium by 5-10 mmol/kg through osmotic diuresis and protein catabolism. Serum potassium at presentation is paradoxically normal or high because acidosis shifts potassium extracellularly (H+/K+ exchange) and insulin deficiency abolishes intracellular uptake. Insulin therapy reverses both, dropping serum potassium rapidly. Without proactive replacement, dangerous hypokalaemia develops within hours. The 40 mmol/L per litre rate matches typical infusion rates of 1 L/h, delivering 40 mmol/h - the upper limit safe via peripheral cannula on cardiac-monitored bed.

sources.label: NSW Health Adult DKA management guideline
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Diabetic ketoacidosis

stem: An 82-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus on metformin and gliclazide is brought by family who report 5 days of progressive lethargy, polyuria and reduced oral intake during a urinary tract infection. She is drowsy and dry. Heart rate 108, blood pressure 102/64, capillary refill 4 seconds. Investigations: capillary glucose 42.6 mmol/L, capillary ketones 0.8 mmol/L. Venous blood gas: pH 7.34, HCO3 22, base excess -2, lactate 2.4. Sodium 158, potassium 4.0, urea 22, creatinine 198 micromol/L. Calculated serum osmolality 348 mOsm/kg. No focal neurology.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate distinction and management priority?

options.text: Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state; slow fluid replacement with sodium chloride 0.9% titrated to gradual osmolality reduction
options.rationale: Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (HHS) is defined by hyperglycaemia >30 mmol/L, hyperosmolality >320 mOsm/kg, absent significant ketosis (ketones <3 mmol/L) and absent acidosis (pH >7.3, HCO3 >15). Management priority is slow fluid replacement; rapid osmolality correction risks cerebral oedema and central pontine myelinolysis. Insulin is added only when fluid alone is not driving glucose down (typically after 1-2 L).
source_refs: NSW Health Adult Hyperglycaemic Emergencies guideline
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - HHS

options.text: Diabetic ketoacidosis; start insulin infusion 0.1 unit/kg/h and rapid fluid
options.rationale: This is not DKA: ketones are low (0.8 vs threshold 3 mmol/L), pH is normal (7.34), HCO3 is normal (22). DKA protocol with rapid fluids and immediate insulin in HHS risks profound hypotension, hypokalaemia and cerebral oedema (NSW Health).
source_refs: NSW Health Adult Hyperglycaemic Emergencies guideline

options.text: Euglycaemic ketoacidosis from SGLT2 inhibitor; investigate medication history
options.rationale: Glucose 42 mmol/L rules out euglycaemic DKA (which presents with normal or near-normal glucose). The patient is also not on an SGLT2 inhibitor (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - DKA

options.text: Acute kidney injury from osmotic diuresis; haemodialysis to lower glucose and osmolality
options.rationale: AKI is present but is a consequence of HHS dehydration and is managed by volume restoration. Dialysis is unnecessary and inappropriately rapid for osmolality correction (NSW Health).
source_refs: NSW Health Adult Hyperglycaemic Emergencies guideline

options.text: Sepsis-driven hyperglycaemia; broad-spectrum antibiotics and source control take priority over glucose
options.rationale: UTI is the precipitant and needs treatment, but the metabolic emergency itself (severe hyperosmolality, dehydration, AKI) drives morbidity. Both must be addressed in parallel; the dominant immediate task is the metabolic correction (NSW Health).
source_refs: NSW Health Adult Hyperglycaemic Emergencies guideline

explanation.summary: HHS and DKA differ in pathophysiology, presentation and treatment. HHS develops over days in older type 2 diabetics with relative (not absolute) insulin deficiency, allowing enough insulin to suppress ketogenesis but not glucose. Treatment priority is slow fluid replacement; insulin is delayed and given at lower rates (0.05 unit/kg/h) to avoid rapid osmolar shifts.
key_points: HHS criteria: BGL >30, osmolality >320, ketones <3, pH >7.3, HCO3 >15
Mortality 10-20% (vs 1-5% DKA); often elderly with comorbidity
Fluid first; insulin only when glucose plateaus on fluid alone (0.05 unit/kg/h)
Cap osmolality reduction at 3-8 mOsm/kg/h to avoid cerebral oedema
explanation.context: HHS occurs when residual insulin (sufficient to suppress ketogenesis but not gluconeogenesis) is overwhelmed by an intercurrent illness. Days of osmotic diuresis produce severe dehydration (typically 100-200 mL/kg deficit) and hyperosmolality. The slow speed of correction matters because rapid water shift into the relatively hyperosmolar brain causes cerebral oedema (a feared paediatric DKA complication that also occurs in HHS adults). Mortality is higher than DKA largely from underlying comorbidities (cardiovascular, renal, infection) and from complications of correction itself when done too quickly.

sources.label: NSW Health Adult Hyperglycaemic Emergencies guideline
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state

stem: A 46-year-old woman with known Addison disease on hydrocortisone 20 mg morning and 10 mg afternoon plus fludrocortisone 100 mcg daily presents to the emergency department with 36 hours of vomiting from gastroenteritis. She is drowsy, hypotensive and hyperpigmented. Heart rate 128, blood pressure 78/42 mmHg unresponsive to 500 mL of compound sodium lactate. Capillary blood glucose 2.8 mmol/L. Sodium 128, potassium 5.8, urea 14, creatinine 142 micromol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 128, BP 78/42 after 500 mL CSL, GCS 13.
data_table.Background: Addison disease, on hydrocortisone 20/10 mg + fludrocortisone 100 mcg, vomiting 36 h.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Hydrocortisone 100 mg intravenously plus 1 L 0.9% saline with 5% glucose
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis is a clinical diagnosis (hypotension, hypoglycaemia, hyperkalaemia, hyponatraemia in a known Addisonian under stress). Immediate management is hydrocortisone 100 mg IV (or IM if no access) plus 1 L 0.9% saline with 5% glucose to correct volume, hypoglycaemia and electrolytes. Treatment is given before biochemical confirmation; cortisol can be sent but should not delay therapy.
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia Adrenal Crisis position
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Adrenal insufficiency

options.text: Dexamethasone 4 mg intravenously and await random cortisol result
options.rationale: Dexamethasone is acceptable when a Short Synacthen test is planned afterwards (it does not interfere with the cortisol assay) but lacks mineralocorticoid activity essential in primary adrenal failure. Hydrocortisone is preferred in established Addison disease (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Adrenal insufficiency

options.text: Noradrenaline infusion via central line for refractory hypotension
options.rationale: Vasopressors are largely ineffective in adrenal crisis without glucocorticoid replacement because the vascular smooth muscle is refractory to catecholamines in absence of cortisol. Hydrocortisone restores vascular reactivity (permissive effect) (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Adrenal insufficiency

options.text: Double the patient's usual oral hydrocortisone and continue oral fludrocortisone
options.rationale: Oral therapy is unreliable in vomiting patient and inadequate for crisis. Parenteral hydrocortisone is required (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Adrenal insufficiency

options.text: Insulin and glucose bolus to manage hyperkalaemia urgently
options.rationale: Hyperkalaemia in adrenal crisis reflects mineralocorticoid deficiency and resolves with hydrocortisone and saline. Insulin-glucose would worsen hypoglycaemia and is not first-line in this context (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Adrenal insufficiency

explanation.summary: Adrenal crisis is treated empirically: hydrocortisone 100 mg IV/IM immediately, plus 1 L 0.9% saline with 5% glucose, then 200 mg/24 h hydrocortisone by continuous infusion or 50 mg 6-hourly. Fludrocortisone is not needed acutely because high-dose hydrocortisone provides sufficient mineralocorticoid activity.
key_points: Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV/IM stat; do not wait for biochemical confirmation
1 L 0.9% saline + 5% glucose corrects volume, sodium, glucose
Hydrocortisone at >50 mg/day provides adequate mineralocorticoid activity
Sick-day rules: double oral hydrocortisone for fever/infection; IM injection at home if vomiting
explanation.context: Cortisol has a permissive effect on vascular tone: alpha-adrenoceptors require cortisol for normal catecholamine responsiveness. Without it, vasopressors fail and hypotension persists despite fluid. Cortisol also opposes insulin action (so deficiency causes hypoglycaemia), and aldosterone deficiency causes sodium loss and potassium retention. The hyperpigmentation of primary adrenal failure reflects ACTH oversecretion: ACTH and melanocyte-stimulating hormone share a common precursor (proopiomelanocortin), so ACTH excess stimulates melanocytes in addition to the failing adrenal.

sources.label: Endocrine Society of Australia Adrenal Crisis position statement
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Adrenal insufficiency

stem: A 38-year-old man with secondary adrenal insufficiency following pituitary adenoma resection 2 years ago is on hydrocortisone 15 mg morning and 5 mg afternoon. He calls the endocrine clinic at the start of an influenza-like illness with fever 38.7 degrees Celsius and myalgia but is tolerating oral fluids and small meals.

data_table.Background: Secondary adrenal insufficiency post pituitary surgery, on hydrocortisone 15/5 mg.
data_table.Current illness: Influenza-like illness, fever 38.7, myalgia, tolerating oral intake.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate sick day advice?

options.text: Double oral hydrocortisone (30 mg morning, 10 mg afternoon) for the duration of fever
options.rationale: Endocrine Society of Australia sick day rules: double oral hydrocortisone for febrile illness, surgery or significant physical stress, then taper back over 24-48 hours as recovery progresses. Tripling is reserved for major stress (surgery, trauma). Pre-loaded IM hydrocortisone is for vomiting or unwell-to-the-point-of-collapse, not yet warranted here.
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia Adrenal Crisis prevention
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Adrenal insufficiency

options.text: Continue usual dose and only escalate if symptoms worsen significantly
options.rationale: Failure to escalate during febrile illness is the commonest precipitant of adrenal crisis in known adrenal insufficiency. Pre-emptive doubling is protective (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia Adrenal Crisis prevention

options.text: Inject intramuscular hydrocortisone 100 mg immediately and present to emergency
options.rationale: IM hydrocortisone and emergency presentation are reserved for vomiting, inability to tolerate oral intake, drowsiness, or hypotension. With preserved oral intake and stable haemodynamics, oral doubling is sufficient (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia Adrenal Crisis prevention

options.text: Switch to prednisone 10 mg daily for simpler dosing during illness
options.rationale: Switching to prednisone (longer half-life, less mineralocorticoid activity) is not required and removes the diurnal physiological pattern of hydrocortisone dosing. Maintain hydrocortisone and double the dose (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Adrenal insufficiency

options.text: Add fludrocortisone 100 mcg daily during illness
options.rationale: Fludrocortisone is mineralocorticoid replacement for primary adrenal insufficiency only. In secondary (pituitary) failure the adrenal cortex still responds to angiotensin II, so aldosterone secretion is preserved. Fludrocortisone is not added (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Adrenal insufficiency

explanation.summary: Sick day rules: double oral hydrocortisone for febrile illness or moderate physical stress; triple for severe illness; intramuscular hydrocortisone 100 mg for vomiting, diarrhoea, collapse or surgery. Patients (and a household member) should carry IM hydrocortisone and a medical alert bracelet.
key_points: Double oral for fever/infection (sick day rule)
IM hydrocortisone 100 mg + ED if vomiting, drowsy, hypotensive
Major surgery: 100 mg IV at induction, then infusion 200 mg/24 h
Primary AI needs fludrocortisone; secondary AI does not
explanation.context: Cortisol secretion normally rises 5-10 fold during physical stress (infection, trauma, surgery), but adrenal-insufficient patients on replacement doses cannot mount this response. Without temporary dose escalation, the gap between physiological demand and replacement supply precipitates crisis. Education is the single most effective preventive measure: patients with adrenal insufficiency who understand sick day rules and carry IM hydrocortisone have substantially fewer crises and hospital admissions.

sources.label: Endocrine Society of Australia Adrenal Crisis Prevention guidance
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Adrenal insufficiency

subtopic_detail: Thyroid storm - recognition and pharmacology sequence
stem: A 32-year-old woman with poorly controlled Graves disease presents to emergency 4 days after stopping carbimazole because of a presumed allergy. She is agitated, confused, tremulous and diaphoretic. Temperature 39.8 degrees Celsius, heart rate 168 atrial fibrillation, blood pressure 162/88, respiratory rate 28. Free thyroxine (T4) 64 pmol/L, free triiodothyronine (T3) 24 pmol/L, thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) <0.01 mU/L. Burch-Wartofsky Point Scale (BWPS) is 65, consistent with thyroid storm.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.8, HR 168 AF, BP 162/88, RR 28, agitated/confused.
data_table.Thyroid: fT4 64 pmol/L, fT3 24 pmol/L, TSH <0.01 mU/L.
data_table.Background: Graves disease, stopped carbimazole 4 days ago (presumed allergy).

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate sequence of pharmacological intervention?

options.text: Propylthiouracil 200 mg orally, then 1 hour later potassium iodide oral solution, plus propranolol and hydrocortisone
options.rationale: Thyroid storm pharmacology has a strict sequence: thionamide first (propylthiouracil preferred over carbimazole because PTU also blocks peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion), then iodide at least 1 hour later (premature iodide before thionamide blockade would worsen the storm via the Jod-Basedow effect), plus propranolol (rate control and inhibits peripheral conversion) and hydrocortisone (blocks T4-to-T3 and treats relative adrenal insufficiency).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia thyroid storm position
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Thyrotoxicosis

options.text: Potassium iodide oral solution immediately, then thionamide 1 hour later
options.rationale: Giving iodide before thionamide allows the thyroid to use the iodide load to synthesise additional thyroid hormone (Jod-Basedow effect), acutely worsening the storm. Thionamide must precede iodide by at least 1 hour (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia thyroid storm position

options.text: Total thyroidectomy after rapid optimisation with beta-blockade
options.rationale: Surgery is contraindicated during active thyroid storm because of haemodynamic instability and uncontrolled hormone release on manipulation. It is the definitive treatment after metabolic stabilisation (typically several weeks) (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Thyrotoxicosis

options.text: Radioactive iodine ablation as immediate definitive therapy
options.rationale: Radioactive iodine causes a transient release of preformed hormone with potential worsening of thyrotoxicosis in the first 1-2 weeks. It is contraindicated during storm (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Thyrotoxicosis

options.text: Carbimazole 60 mg orally with concurrent ibuprofen for pyrexia
options.rationale: PTU is preferred in thyroid storm because it inhibits peripheral T4 to T3 conversion (carbimazole does not). Aspirin and other NSAIDs that displace thyroid hormone from binding proteins should be avoided; paracetamol is the antipyretic of choice (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia thyroid storm position

explanation.summary: Thyroid storm management combines blockade of hormone synthesis (thionamide), release (iodide given >=1 hour after thionamide), peripheral conversion (propranolol, hydrocortisone, PTU), and sympathetic effects (propranolol). Active cooling with paracetamol (not aspirin/NSAIDs) and supportive care complete the package.
key_points: PTU preferred over carbimazole (also blocks peripheral T4 to T3)
Iodide always after thionamide by >=1 hour (Jod-Basedow risk)
Hydrocortisone: blocks T4 to T3, treats relative adrenal insufficiency
Propranolol: rate control and blocks peripheral conversion at high doses
explanation.context: Thyroid storm is decompensated thyrotoxicosis with a mortality of 10-30%. The Wolff-Chaikoff effect describes iodide-induced suppression of thyroid hormone synthesis (the therapeutic intent), but in untreated Graves disease the failure to escape from this effect is unreliable, and iodide can paradoxically increase hormone synthesis through the Jod-Basedow effect if thyroid peroxidase is not first blocked by thionamide. This is why sequence matters: PTU or carbimazole first to block synthesis, then iodide to block release of preformed hormone.

sources.label: Endocrine Society of Australia thyroid storm position statement
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Thyrotoxicosis

stem: An 80-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department in winter after being found unresponsive at home. She has known hypothyroidism but is reported to have stopped levothyroxine some months ago because she 'felt fine'. She is hypothermic and obtunded. Temperature 31.4 degrees Celsius (rectal), heart rate 42, blood pressure 86/52, respiratory rate 8 and shallow. Sodium 122, potassium 4.4, glucose 3.1, cortisol 198 nmol/L (random). TSH 102 mU/L, free T4 3 pmol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: T 31.4 (rectal), HR 42, BP 86/52, RR 8 shallow, GCS 6.
data_table.Bloods: Na 122, K 4.4, glucose 3.1, random cortisol 198 nmol/L.
data_table.Background: Known hypothyroidism, stopped levothyroxine months ago, winter.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg, then intravenous levothyroxine 200-400 mcg loading
options.rationale: Myxoedema coma management: give intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg BEFORE thyroid hormone, because untreated coexisting adrenal insufficiency unmasked by thyroid hormone replacement can precipitate adrenal crisis (cortisol metabolism accelerates as thyroid status normalises). Random cortisol of 198 nmol/L in a critically ill patient is inappropriately low (would expect >500 in true stress). Levothyroxine 200-400 mcg IV loading dose follows.
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia myxoedema coma position
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hypothyroidism

options.text: Intravenous levothyroxine 500 mcg loading, then hydrocortisone if cortisol low
options.rationale: Giving thyroid hormone before glucocorticoid in a patient with possible coexisting adrenal insufficiency can precipitate adrenal crisis through accelerated cortisol metabolism. Hydrocortisone empirically first is the safer sequence (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia myxoedema coma position

options.text: Active external rewarming with forced-air blanket and observation
options.rationale: Passive rewarming is preferred in myxoedema coma; aggressive external rewarming causes peripheral vasodilatation, worsening hypotension and triggering arrhythmia. Endogenous heat generation as metabolism recovers with thyroid replacement is the optimal mechanism (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia myxoedema coma position

options.text: Oral levothyroxine 200 mcg via nasogastric tube and supportive care
options.rationale: Gut absorption is unreliable in myxoedema coma (gastroparesis, mucosal oedema, hypoperfusion). IV route is required to ensure absorption (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia myxoedema coma position

options.text: Hypertonic 3% saline correction for severe hyponatraemia
options.rationale: The hyponatraemia in myxoedema reflects reduced free water clearance and resolves slowly with thyroid replacement. Hypertonic saline risks rapid correction and central pontine myelinolysis. Reserve for symptomatic seizures from hyponatraemia, not for hyponatraemia attributable to myxoedema (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia myxoedema coma position

explanation.summary: Myxoedema coma combines profound hypothyroidism with hypothermia, hyponatraemia, hypoventilation, bradycardia and altered consciousness. Treatment: empirical hydrocortisone first (cover possible adrenal insufficiency), then IV levothyroxine loading, passive rewarming, supportive care. Mortality remains 30-50% even with treatment.
key_points: Hydrocortisone BEFORE thyroid hormone (sequence matters)
IV levothyroxine 200-400 mcg load then 50-100 mcg daily IV
Passive rewarming only; active worsens hypotension
Liothyronine (T3) sometimes added; debate over routine use
explanation.context: Myxoedema coma is decompensated severe hypothyroidism, classically in elderly women in winter after a precipitant (infection, cold exposure, drug, medication non-adherence). Sodium-retaining steroids and free water excretion both depend on thyroid hormone; deficiency produces dilutional hyponatraemia. Hypothermia reflects basal metabolic rate collapse. The adrenal-thyroid interaction matters because thyroid hormone accelerates cortisol clearance, so replacing T4 without first covering with hydrocortisone in someone with marginal adrenal reserve can precipitate adrenal crisis hours into treatment.

sources.label: Endocrine Society of Australia myxoedema coma position statement
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hypothyroidism

stem: A 72-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department after a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home. She has had progressive lethargy and confusion over 5 days. She is on hydrochlorothiazide for hypertension and was started on a thiazide-sparing antidepressant (escitalopram) 2 weeks ago. Heart rate 88, blood pressure 138/84, post-ictal Glasgow Coma Score 12. Sodium 108 mmol/L, potassium 3.6, urea 4.2, creatinine 64 micromol/L. Serum osmolality 232 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 480 mOsm/kg, urine sodium 58 mmol/L. Euvolaemic on examination.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Hypertonic 3% saline 150 mL bolus over 20 minutes, repeat up to twice if symptoms persist, aiming sodium rise of 4-6 mmol/L in first hour
options.rationale: Severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizure, coma) regardless of chronicity is treated with hypertonic 3% saline 150 mL bolus over 10-20 minutes, repeated up to 3 times until symptoms resolve. Target sodium rise is 4-6 mmol/L in the first hour to abort cerebral oedema, then cap at <=8 mmol/L in the first 24 hours overall to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome.
source_refs: European Society of Endocrinology Hyponatraemia guidelines (referenced by RACP/Endocrine Society of Australia)
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyponatraemia

options.text: 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L over 4 hours and fluid restriction to 1 L/24 h
options.rationale: 0.9% saline in a SIADH-pattern patient (urine osm > serum osm, urine Na high, euvolaemic) can paradoxically worsen hyponatraemia because the sodium is excreted with retained water. Symptomatic seizure also requires faster correction than slow saline allows (ESE/eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyponatraemia

options.text: Fluid restriction to 800 mL/day plus oral salt tablets
options.rationale: Fluid restriction is appropriate for chronic, mildly symptomatic hyponatraemia. Severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizure) needs immediate hypertonic saline; restriction acts too slowly (ESE).
source_refs: European Society of Endocrinology Hyponatraemia guidelines

options.text: Tolvaptan 15 mg orally daily and monitor sodium 6-hourly
options.rationale: Tolvaptan (vasopressin V2 antagonist) is contraindicated in acute severe symptomatic hyponatraemia because its onset is too slow and correction can be unpredictable. Reserved for chronic SIADH (ESE).
source_refs: European Society of Endocrinology Hyponatraemia guidelines

options.text: Demeclocycline 600 mg orally daily for chronic SIADH management
options.rationale: Demeclocycline (tetracycline that induces nephrogenic diabetes insipidus) is a chronic SIADH management option, not acute treatment. Inappropriate for symptomatic hyponatraemia (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyponatraemia

explanation.summary: Acute severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizure, coma) is an emergency treated with 3% hypertonic saline 150 mL bolus, repeated as needed for symptom resolution. Target sodium rise 4-6 mmol/L in the first hour, then capped at <=8 mmol/L in 24 hours and <=18 mmol/L in 48 hours to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome.
key_points: Hypertonic 3% saline 150 mL bolus over 10-20 min; up to 3 boluses
Target: +4-6 mmol/L in first hour for seizure/coma; symptom abortion is the endpoint
Then cap: <=8 mmol/L per 24 h, <=18 mmol/L per 48 h
If over-correction: re-lower with dextrose 5% and/or desmopressin
explanation.context: Osmotic demyelination syndrome (formerly central pontine myelinolysis) occurs when chronically adapted hypotonic brain tissue is exposed to rapid osmotic change. Brain cells lose intracellular osmolytes (organic osmolytes, electrolytes) over 24-48 hours to match the hypotonic extracellular fluid, preventing cerebral oedema. Rapid correction overwhelms the slow re-accumulation of these osmolytes, drawing water out of cells faster than they can re-adapt. Pontine oligodendrocytes are particularly susceptible to osmotic flux, producing the characteristic 'locked-in' syndrome 2-6 days after over-correction. Risk factors: chronicity, hypokalaemia, malnutrition, alcohol use disorder, liver disease.

sources.label: European Society of Endocrinology Hyponatraemia guidelines (cited by Endocrine Society of Australia)
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyponatraemia

stem: A 64-year-old man is reviewed in the outpatient clinic for incidentally noted sodium of 124 mmol/L on routine bloods. He has small cell lung carcinoma diagnosed 3 weeks ago, recently commenced platinum-based chemotherapy. He is asymptomatic. He is clinically euvolaemic. Serum osmolality 258 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 520 mOsm/kg, urine sodium 62 mmol/L. Thyroid stimulating hormone 1.8 mU/L, morning cortisol 380 nmol/L.

data_table.Background: Small cell lung carcinoma, on chemotherapy.

lead_in: What is the most likely cause of his hyponatraemia?

options.text: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion from small cell lung carcinoma
options.rationale: Hypotonic hyponatraemia (serum osm <275) with inappropriately concentrated urine (urine osm >100), urine sodium >40 mmol/L, euvolaemic clinical state, and normal thyroid and adrenal function defines SIADH. Small cell lung carcinoma is the prototypical malignancy producing ectopic antidiuretic hormone (ADH).
source_refs: European Society of Endocrinology Hyponatraemia guidelines
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyponatraemia

options.text: Pseudohyponatraemia from hyperlipidaemia
options.rationale: Pseudohyponatraemia gives a falsely low sodium with normal measured serum osmolality. Here serum osmolality is genuinely low (258), so this is true hypotonic hyponatraemia (ESE).
source_refs: European Society of Endocrinology Hyponatraemia guidelines

options.text: Hypovolaemic hyponatraemia from chemotherapy-induced vomiting
options.rationale: Hypovolaemic hyponatraemia features clinical hypovolaemia (which he does not have) and low urine sodium (<20 mmol/L) reflecting aldosterone-driven sodium retention. His urine Na is 62 (ESE).
source_refs: European Society of Endocrinology Hyponatraemia guidelines

options.text: Hypothyroidism-related impaired free water clearance
options.rationale: TSH is normal (1.8), excluding significant hypothyroidism as the cause (ESE).
source_refs: European Society of Endocrinology Hyponatraemia guidelines

options.text: Adrenal insufficiency with cortisol deficiency
options.rationale: Morning cortisol of 380 nmol/L is reassuringly within normal range. Random cortisol >275 in non-stressed patient usually excludes primary adrenal insufficiency (ESE).
source_refs: European Society of Endocrinology Hyponatraemia guidelines

explanation.summary: Hyponatraemia workup proceeds through serum osmolality (true vs pseudo), volume status (hypo/eu/hyper), and urine indices (urine osmolality and sodium). Euvolaemic hypotonic hyponatraemia with high urine osmolality and high urine sodium, after excluding thyroid and adrenal causes, is SIADH. Small cell lung carcinoma is the classical malignant cause.
key_points: Always check serum osm first to exclude pseudo (lipid, protein) and hyperosmolar (glucose) hyponatraemia
SIADH criteria: hypotonic, urine osm >100, urine Na >40, euvolaemic, normal TFT/adrenal/renal
SCLC, head injury, drugs (SSRIs, carbamazepine), pneumonia: common SIADH causes
Treat underlying cause; fluid restrict 800-1000 mL/day; tolvaptan/demeclocycline for chronic
explanation.context: ADH is secreted by the posterior pituitary in response to plasma osmolality (rising) and effective circulating volume (falling). In SIADH, ADH secretion is uncoupled from these stimuli, often from ectopic production (SCLC), CNS pathology (head injury, infection), pulmonary disease (pneumonia, lung abscess), or drugs. Persistent ADH action drives free water retention through aquaporin-2 channels in the collecting duct, diluting serum sodium without volume expansion (escape from the volume effect through pressure natriuresis prevents oedema).

sources.label: European Society of Endocrinology Hyponatraemia guidelines
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyponatraemia

stem: A 68-year-old man with chronic kidney disease stage 4 (estimated glomerular filtration rate 22), heart failure on perindopril and spironolactone, presents to a Central Coast LHD emergency department with weakness. Heart rate 48, blood pressure 142/82. Electrocardiogram shows peaked T waves throughout the precordial leads and PR prolongation to 220 ms. Potassium 7.4 mmol/L (from venous blood gas, confirmed on formal serum 7.2), sodium 132, urea 28, creatinine 384 micromol/L, ionised calcium 1.18 mmol/L.

data_table.ECG: Peaked T waves precordial leads, PR 220 ms.
data_table.Bloods: K 7.4 (VBG), K 7.2 (serum), Na 132, urea 28, creatinine 384, ionised Ca 1.18.
data_table.Background: CKD stage 4 (eGFR 22), HF, on perindopril + spironolactone.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate single first intervention?

options.text: Calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL intravenously over 5 minutes
options.rationale: ECG changes (peaked T waves, PR prolongation) with K >6.5 mmol/L mandate immediate cardiac membrane stabilisation with intravenous calcium. Calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL or calcium chloride 10% 5 mL over 5 minutes antagonises the depolarising effect of potassium on cardiac membranes within 1-3 minutes. Effect lasts ~30-60 minutes; repeat if ECG changes persist.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyperkalaemia
Kidney Health Australia CARI - Hyperkalaemia

options.text: Insulin 10 units intravenously with 50 mL of 50% glucose
options.rationale: Insulin-glucose drives potassium intracellularly over 15-30 minutes but does not protect the myocardium during the lag. Calcium first when ECG changes are present, then insulin-glucose (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyperkalaemia

options.text: Salbutamol 10 mg nebulised
options.rationale: Nebulised salbutamol shifts potassium intracellularly via beta-2 stimulation, with onset ~30 minutes. Useful as an adjunct but does not protect the myocardium. Calcium first (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyperkalaemia

options.text: Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Resonium) 30 g orally
options.rationale: Cation exchange resins work over hours through gut excretion and do not address the acute cardiac risk. Adverse events include intestinal necrosis; use limited (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyperkalaemia

options.text: Sodium bicarbonate 8.4% 50 mL intravenously
options.rationale: Bicarbonate has limited utility in non-acidotic hyperkalaemia. Risks include sodium loading, hypocalcaemia and pulmonary oedema in CKD. Not first-line in cardiac emergency (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyperkalaemia

explanation.summary: Severe hyperkalaemia management follows a stabilise-shift-eliminate sequence: calcium gluconate or chloride for membrane stabilisation (immediate, 30-60 min duration), insulin-glucose and/or salbutamol for intracellular shift (15-30 min onset, hours duration), then elimination via dialysis or cation exchange resin. Definitive treatment is removal of cause and renal/extracorporeal clearance.
key_points: Calcium first when ECG changes or K >6.5: stabilises myocardium
Insulin 10 units + 50 mL 50% glucose: shifts K intracellularly
Salbutamol 10-20 mg nebulised: adjunct shift
Stop offending drugs: ACEi, ARB, MRA, NSAIDs, K-sparing diuretics, trimethoprim
explanation.context: Cardiac toxicity in hyperkalaemia progresses through peaked T waves, PR prolongation, P wave loss, QRS widening into a sinusoidal pattern, and finally ventricular fibrillation or asystole. Calcium does not lower potassium but raises the threshold potential of cardiomyocytes, restoring the difference between resting and threshold potentials that hyperkalaemia narrows. Calcium chloride contains 3x the calcium per millilitre of calcium gluconate but is more sclerosing and requires central access; gluconate is the peripheral default.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyperkalaemia
Kidney Health Australia CARI - Hyperkalaemia management

stem: A 74-year-old woman with hypertension and chronic kidney disease stage 3b (estimated glomerular filtration rate 38) presents with serum potassium 6.4 mmol/L. She takes the following: perindopril 4 mg, spironolactone 25 mg, amlodipine 5 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, paracetamol 1 g 6-hourly, ibuprofen 400 mg 8-hourly (started last week for knee pain), trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole (commenced 4 days ago for a urinary tract infection).

data_table.Recent additions: Ibuprofen 1 week ago; trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole 4 days ago for UTI.

lead_in: Which combination of medications has most likely contributed to the hyperkalaemia?

options.text: Perindopril, spironolactone, ibuprofen, trimethoprim
options.rationale: Four hyperkalaemia-promoting drugs are present and compounded by CKD. ACE inhibitor (perindopril) reduces aldosterone; potassium-sparing diuretic (spironolactone) blocks aldosterone receptor; NSAID (ibuprofen) reduces renal perfusion and aldosterone; trimethoprim blocks epithelial sodium channels in the distal tubule (amiloride-like effect). Each independently raises potassium; together in CKD they cause clinically significant hyperkalaemia within days.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyperkalaemia
Australian Medicines Handbook

options.text: Atorvastatin and paracetamol
options.rationale: Neither atorvastatin nor paracetamol meaningfully affects potassium. Atorvastatin has a small risk of rhabdomyolysis-related hyperkalaemia but not at this dose, and paracetamol does not affect potassium handling (AMH).
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook

options.text: Amlodipine and atorvastatin
options.rationale: Calcium channel blockers and statins do not raise potassium (AMH).
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook

options.text: Perindopril and atorvastatin
options.rationale: Perindopril alone is correct but missing the major contributors of spironolactone, NSAID and trimethoprim, all of which compound the effect (AMH).
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook

options.text: Spironolactone and amlodipine
options.rationale: Spironolactone is correct but amlodipine does not contribute to hyperkalaemia. Missing the other three contributors (AMH).
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook

explanation.summary: Drug-induced hyperkalaemia in CKD typically reflects compounding of multiple agents acting on renal potassium handling. Key offenders: ACE inhibitors/ARBs (reduce aldosterone), potassium-sparing diuretics (block aldosterone receptor or sodium channels), NSAIDs (reduce renal perfusion), trimethoprim (amiloride-like sodium-channel block), heparin and ciclosporin/tacrolimus (reduce aldosterone).
key_points: ACE/ARB: reduce aldosterone via renin-angiotensin blockade
MRA (spironolactone, eplerenone): block aldosterone receptor
NSAIDs: vasoconstrict afferent arteriole, reduce GFR and aldosterone
Trimethoprim, amiloride: block epithelial sodium channels (amiloride-like)
explanation.context: Trimethoprim deserves particular note because it is widely prescribed for urinary tract infection and frequently overlooked as a cause of hyperkalaemia. Its structural similarity to amiloride blocks the epithelial sodium channel in the distal tubule, reducing the favourable electrochemical gradient that normally drives potassium secretion. In patients already on RAAS-blocking drugs and with reduced GFR, even a short course can precipitate clinically significant hyperkalaemia. The mechanism is dose-dependent and more pronounced with the higher doses used for Pneumocystis treatment than UTI prophylaxis, but UTI treatment doses are sufficient to provoke this when other contributors are present.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyperkalaemia
Australian Medicines Handbook

stem: A 54-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus on metformin, empagliflozin and sitagliptin presents the day after laparoscopic cholecystectomy with 12 hours of progressive nausea, abdominal pain and dyspnoea. He fasted for 18 hours before surgery and has eaten little since. Capillary blood glucose 8.4 mmol/L, capillary ketones 4.8 mmol/L. Venous blood gas: pH 7.16, HCO3 10, base excess -16, lactate 2.1. Sodium 137, potassium 4.2, chloride 102, anion gap 25.

data_table.Background: T2DM on metformin + empagliflozin + sitagliptin, post-op day 1 cholecystectomy, 18 h fast.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Treat as DKA with insulin-dextrose infusion plus saline, withhold empagliflozin
options.rationale: SGLT2 inhibitor-associated euglycaemic DKA presents with anion-gap acidosis and elevated ketones at near-normal glucose. The empagliflozin lowers renal glucose threshold, suppressing the hyperglycaemia signal that would normally prompt earlier diagnosis. Treatment is identical to standard DKA except glucose-containing fluids are needed from the start to permit insulin without hypoglycaemia. Withhold SGLT2i immediately and indefinitely until cause investigated.
source_refs: TGA SGLT2 inhibitor safety alert (euglycaemic DKA)
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Diabetic ketoacidosis

options.text: Treat as starvation ketosis with intravenous glucose alone
options.rationale: Starvation ketosis produces mild ketonaemia (1-2 mmol/L) without significant acidosis (pH >7.3, HCO3 >18). Ketones of 4.8 and pH 7.16 indicate DKA-level ketogenesis, requiring insulin not just glucose (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Diabetic ketoacidosis

options.text: Investigate as post-operative sepsis and start broad-spectrum antibiotics
options.rationale: Sepsis can precipitate DKA but the metabolic emergency is the immediate threat. Lactate 2.1 is mildly elevated but the dominant abnormality is ketoacidosis. Sepsis workup runs in parallel with DKA treatment, not instead (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Diabetic ketoacidosis

options.text: Continue empagliflozin and add NPH insulin twice daily
options.rationale: SGLT2 inhibitor MUST be withheld during DKA; continuing perpetuates the precipitant. Sliding-scale subcutaneous insulin is inadequate for established DKA, which needs intravenous insulin infusion (eTG, TGA).
source_refs: TGA SGLT2 inhibitor safety alert (euglycaemic DKA)

options.text: Resume oral metformin and observe for symptom improvement
options.rationale: Oral therapy is unreliable in acutely unwell, nauseated, acidotic patient. Established DKA requires IV insulin and fluid (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Diabetic ketoacidosis

explanation.summary: Euglycaemic DKA on SGLT2 inhibitor presents with high ketones and acidosis at near-normal glucose; the SGLT2 inhibitor lowers glucose by promoting glycosuria, masking the typical hyperglycaemia. Triggers include fasting, surgery, low carbohydrate intake, and acute illness. Treat as DKA with insulin and glucose-containing fluids; withhold SGLT2i pre- and post-operatively (TGA advises stopping at least 3 days before elective surgery).
key_points: BGL may be near-normal; ketones high; acidosis present
Withhold SGLT2i at least 3 days before elective surgery (TGA)
Insulin + dextrose infusion (glucose 10% if BGL near-normal at outset)
Reintroduction of SGLT2i only after careful review and risk discussion
explanation.context: SGLT2 inhibitors increase ketogenesis by promoting fatty acid oxidation (glucagon rises, insulin falls relatively in fasting or stressed states) and reduce renal ketone clearance. Their glycosuric effect lowers plasma glucose, suppressing the usual feedback that would prompt insulin dose increase. The result is sufficient insulin deficiency to drive ketogenesis without the warning signal of hyperglycaemia. The clinical pearl is to check ketones in any SGLT2i patient with abdominal pain, vomiting or dyspnoea even when BGL is normal.

sources.label: TGA SGLT2 inhibitor safety alert - euglycaemic DKA
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Diabetic ketoacidosis

stem: A 15-year-old girl is being managed for severe DKA in a HNE LHD emergency department. After 4 hours of treatment (3 L 0.9% saline at 1 L/h then 500 mL/h, insulin infusion 5 units/h, potassium replacement, BGL down from 35 to 14 mmol/L), she develops a headache, then becomes drowsy and irritable. Her glasgow coma score has dropped from 15 to 11 in 30 minutes. Heart rate 62 (was 110), blood pressure 152/92 (was 96/56). Pupils equal, briskly reactive.

data_table.Trajectory: Hour 4 of DKA Rx: GCS dropped 15 to 11 over 30 min, headache then drowsiness.
data_table.Fluids: 3 L 0.9% saline (1 L/h x 1 then 500 mL/h x 4), insulin 5 units/h.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Hypertonic 3% saline 5 mL/kg bolus, reduce fluid rate, urgent CT head and paediatric ICU referral
options.rationale: Headache, GCS drop, bradycardia and hypertension (Cushing reflex) in a child being treated for DKA are classic features of cerebral oedema, a feared complication with high mortality. Immediate management is osmotic therapy with hypertonic saline (or mannitol), reduction of fluid rate, and urgent imaging plus PICU. Delay risks brainstem herniation.
source_refs: Australasian Paediatric Endocrine Group DKA guideline (referenced for adolescent management)
Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne DKA Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Increase fluid rate to treat presumed dehydration and worsening shock
options.rationale: Bradycardia and hypertension in this context reflect raised intracranial pressure (Cushing reflex), not hypovolaemia. Increasing fluid would worsen cerebral oedema. The volume status is appropriate after 3 L resuscitation (RCH; APEG).
source_refs: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne DKA Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Increase insulin infusion to 0.15 unit/kg/h to drive glucose down further
options.rationale: Rapid glucose fall is itself a risk factor for cerebral oedema. The current rate of glucose fall (35 to 14 in 4 h) is already at the limit. Glucose should be added to fluids to slow descent below 14 mmol/L (RCH).
source_refs: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne DKA Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Administer sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg to reverse acidosis faster
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is associated with increased cerebral oedema risk in paediatric DKA and is contraindicated outside extreme acidosis with haemodynamic compromise. Would worsen the situation (APEG; RCH).
source_refs: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne DKA Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Give intramuscular promethazine for nausea and reassess in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Drowsy adolescent with bradycardia, hypertension and GCS drop is not nauseous from gastritis; this is a neurological emergency. Antihistamine sedation would mask deterioration (RCH).
source_refs: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne DKA Clinical Practice Guideline

explanation.summary: DKA-related cerebral oedema is rare (~1% of paediatric DKA) but carries 20-30% mortality. Warning signs: headache, GCS drop, bradycardia, hypertension, incontinence, abnormal posturing. Treatment is immediate osmotic therapy (hypertonic 3% saline 5 mL/kg or mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg), reduced fluid rate, imaging, and PICU. Prevention focuses on avoiding excessive fluid (>4 L/m2/24 h), rapid glucose correction and bicarbonate.
key_points: Suspicion: headache, GCS drop, Cushing reflex (brady + HTN), in adolescent on DKA Rx
Hypertonic 3% saline 5 mL/kg OR mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg over 20 min
Reduce maintenance fluid rate by one-third
Cap glucose fall at ~5 mmol/L/h by adding 10% dextrose when BGL <14
explanation.context: The pathophysiology of DKA cerebral oedema is debated. Historical theory: rapid osmotic shift from extracellular to intracellular brain compartment as glucose and ketones fall. Newer theory: ischaemic-reperfusion injury, with cerebral hypoperfusion during DKA followed by hyperaemia on rehydration causing vasogenic oedema. Whatever the mechanism, the practical implications are the same: avoid rapid osmolar swings (cap glucose fall, avoid bicarbonate, do not over-resuscitate), monitor neurological status closely, and act immediately on warning signs. Adolescents are at higher risk than adults but the syndrome occurs in adults too.

sources.label: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne DKA Clinical Practice Guideline
Australasian Paediatric Endocrine Group DKA guideline

stem: A 70-year-old man on amiodarone 200 mg daily for 4 years for atrial fibrillation presents with thyrotoxicosis. TSH <0.01 mU/L, free T4 48 pmol/L, free T3 12 pmol/L. Thyroid antibody panel negative. Colour Doppler thyroid ultrasound shows reduced vascularity with patchy parenchymal changes. Iodine-123 uptake scan shows very low uptake. Interleukin-6 elevated.

data_table.Thyroid: TSH <0.01 mU/L, fT4 48 pmol/L, fT3 12 pmol/L.
data_table.Imaging: Colour Doppler US: reduced vascularity, patchy parenchymal change; I-123 uptake very low.
data_table.Background: 4 years on amiodarone 200 mg/day for AF.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial treatment?

options.text: High-dose oral prednisolone 40-60 mg daily
options.rationale: The picture is amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis type 2: destructive thyroiditis (reduced vascularity, low uptake, raised IL-6, no underlying autoimmunity). Type 2 responds to glucocorticoids which suppress the inflammatory destruction and reduce hormone release. Type 1 (excess iodine-driven synthesis in pre-existing Graves or nodular goitre) responds to thionamides (carbimazole or PTU). Mixed picture sometimes warrants both.
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis guidance
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Thyrotoxicosis

options.text: Carbimazole 40 mg orally daily as first-line
options.rationale: Carbimazole (thionamide) treats type 1 amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis where hormone synthesis is increased. In type 2 (destructive), thionamides are ineffective because hormone release is from stored preformed hormone, not new synthesis (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis guidance

options.text: Total thyroidectomy as immediate definitive therapy
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for refractory cases or where rapid control is needed for cardiac decompensation. Initial medical therapy is appropriate (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis guidance

options.text: Radioactive iodine ablation
options.rationale: Radioactive iodine uptake is very low because of iodine-induced thyroid suppression from amiodarone. RAI is ineffective until iodine pool depletes (months) (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis guidance

options.text: Discontinue amiodarone and observe for spontaneous resolution
options.rationale: Amiodarone has a half-life of ~50-100 days; thyrotoxicosis persists for months after stopping. Treatment must proceed regardless of whether amiodarone is continued (often it cannot be stopped because of underlying arrhythmia). The decision to stop amiodarone is individualised (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis guidance

explanation.summary: Amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis has two types: type 1 (iodine-driven hyperthyroidism in pre-existing thyroid disease) treated with thionamides; type 2 (destructive thyroiditis releasing preformed hormone) treated with glucocorticoids. Discrimination uses vascularity on colour Doppler (increased in type 1, decreased in type 2), uptake scan (low in both but lower in type 2), and inflammatory markers (IL-6 raised in type 2).
key_points: Type 1: pre-existing thyroid disease, raised vascularity, treat with thionamide
Type 2: destructive thyroiditis, low vascularity, raised IL-6, treat with prednisolone
Mixed type: both prednisolone and thionamide
Amiodarone half-life 50-100 days; effects persist long after cessation
explanation.context: Amiodarone is 37% iodine by weight and saturates the thyroid with iodine. In susceptible individuals (pre-existing autonomous nodules or Graves), this drives type 1 thyrotoxicosis through the Jod-Basedow effect (excess iodine substrate). In others, amiodarone or its metabolites have a direct cytotoxic effect on thyroid follicular cells, causing destructive thyroiditis (type 2) with leakage of preformed hormone. The distinction matters therapeutically: thionamides block synthesis (type 1 disease) but cannot affect release of stored hormone (type 2), where glucocorticoid suppression of inflammation is required.

sources.label: Endocrine Society of Australia amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis guidance
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Thyrotoxicosis

stem: A 52-year-old man with a 4 cm right adrenal mass discovered incidentally on CT for renal colic is referred to endocrinology. Plasma metanephrines: metanephrine 1280 pg/mL (reference <88), normetanephrine 2640 pg/mL (reference <196). MIBG scan shows tracer concentration in the right adrenal mass. He has episodic headaches, sweating and palpitations. Blood pressure 168/102 today; previously 145/95 on amlodipine. He is scheduled for laparoscopic adrenalectomy. The surgical team asks about pre-operative preparation.

data_table.Imaging: 4 cm right adrenal mass on CT; MIBG-avid.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate pre-operative pharmacological preparation?

options.text: Commence phenoxybenzamine 10 mg twice daily, uptitrate over 10-14 days, add beta-blocker only after adequate alpha-blockade
options.rationale: Phaeochromocytoma surgery requires alpha-adrenergic blockade for 10-14 days pre-operatively to prevent intraoperative hypertensive crisis from catecholamine release. Phenoxybenzamine (non-selective irreversible alpha-blocker) is first-line; uptitrate to symptom control (postural hypotension on standing indicates adequate blockade). Beta-blocker is added ONLY after alpha-blockade established; giving beta-blocker first allows unopposed alpha vasoconstriction and can precipitate hypertensive crisis or pulmonary oedema.
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia phaeochromocytoma guidance
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Phaeochromocytoma

options.text: Commence metoprolol 50 mg twice daily for rate control
options.rationale: Beta-blockade before alpha-blockade is dangerous: blocks beta-2 mediated vasodilatation, leaving unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction and risk of hypertensive crisis and cardiogenic shock. Always alpha first (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia phaeochromocytoma guidance

options.text: Continue amlodipine and add lisinopril for blood pressure control
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors and calcium-channel blockers do not address the perioperative catecholamine surge risk. Alpha-blockade is the specific pre-operative requirement (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia phaeochromocytoma guidance

options.text: Proceed directly to surgery with intraoperative magnesium and sodium nitroprusside ready
options.rationale: Skipping alpha-blockade exposes the patient to intraoperative hypertensive crisis with stroke and myocardial infarction risk during tumour manipulation. Pre-operative preparation reduces mortality from ~50% historically to <2% modern (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia phaeochromocytoma guidance

options.text: Octreotide infusion for 48 hours pre-operatively
options.rationale: Octreotide is used for somatostatin-receptor-positive neuroendocrine tumours (carcinoid, gastrinoma, glucagonoma) but has no role in phaeochromocytoma where alpha-blockade is required (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia phaeochromocytoma guidance

explanation.summary: Phaeochromocytoma pre-operative preparation: 10-14 days of alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin) titrated to symptom control and mild postural hypotension; high-sodium diet and fluid loading; beta-blocker added only after alpha-blockade for tachycardia; surgery in experienced centre with intraoperative monitoring of haemodynamics. Untreated tumour manipulation can release catecholamine surges of 100-1000x normal.
key_points: Phenoxybenzamine 10 mg bd uptitrated over 10-14 days (or doxazosin)
Beta-blocker AFTER alpha-blockade established (never before)
Volume expansion: liberal fluid intake and salt 5-10 days before surgery
Post-op risks: hypotension from alpha-blockade rebound, hypoglycaemia
explanation.context: The 'alpha-before-beta' rule reflects vascular receptor physiology. Beta-2 receptors mediate vasodilatation in skeletal muscle vasculature; blocking them while alpha-mediated vasoconstriction continues unopposed shifts the balance toward severe vasoconstriction. Add the catecholamine output of an unblocked phaeochromocytoma and a hypertensive crisis follows. Phenoxybenzamine's irreversible non-selective alpha-blockade gives a more durable effect than competitive alpha-1 selective blockers; the duration outlasts catecholamine release peaks during tumour manipulation. The post-operative hypotension that follows tumour removal reflects sudden withdrawal of catecholamine drive against still-active alpha-blockade and prepared low vascular tone.

sources.label: Endocrine Society of Australia phaeochromocytoma guidance
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Phaeochromocytoma

stem: A 67-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer presents to a Central Coast LHD emergency department with confusion, polyuria, polydipsia and constipation. Heart rate 112, blood pressure 102/64. Corrected calcium 3.84 mmol/L (reference 2.10-2.60), phosphate 0.78, parathyroid hormone (PTH) 0.6 pmol/L (reference 1.6-6.9), parathyroid hormone-related peptide (PTHrP) elevated, 25-hydroxyvitamin D 62 nmol/L. Creatinine 142 micromol/L (baseline 78).
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial treatment sequence?

options.text: Aggressive intravenous 0.9% saline rehydration, then intravenous zoledronic acid 4 mg
options.rationale: Severe hypercalcaemia (>3.5 mmol/L) with symptoms is a medical emergency. Initial step is volume expansion with 0.9% saline (3-6 L/24 h) to restore renal perfusion and promote calciuresis. Once volume replete, intravenous bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid 4 mg over 15 minutes) inhibits osteoclast-mediated bone resorption; effect onset 2-4 days, peak 4-7 days, duration weeks. Useful adjuncts: calcitonin (rapid onset, tachyphylaxis), denosumab (refractory cases).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hypercalcaemia
MASCC/ISOO guidelines for malignancy-associated hypercalcaemia

options.text: Frusemide 40 mg intravenously to promote calcium excretion
options.rationale: Routine furosemide in hypercalcaemia is no longer recommended: it exacerbates volume depletion (worsening calcium reabsorption) without providing additional calciuresis once volume is restored. Reserve for fluid overload during saline therapy (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hypercalcaemia

options.text: Oral cinacalcet 30 mg twice daily for symptomatic control
options.rationale: Cinacalcet (calcimimetic) is for primary or tertiary hyperparathyroidism and parathyroid carcinoma. PTH-suppressed humoral hypercalcaemia of malignancy does not respond (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hypercalcaemia

options.text: Prednisolone 60 mg daily for first-line management
options.rationale: Glucocorticoids work for granulomatous (sarcoidosis, TB) or vitamin D-mediated (lymphoma, multiple myeloma in some cases) hypercalcaemia by reducing 1,25(OH)2D production. PTHrP-mediated humoral hypercalcaemia does not respond (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hypercalcaemia

options.text: Phosphate replacement orally to chelate serum calcium
options.rationale: Oral phosphate is reserved for hypophosphataemic hypercalcaemia (rare) and carries risks of metastatic calcification and renal injury at high serum calcium. Not appropriate first-line (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hypercalcaemia

explanation.summary: Hypercalcaemic crisis treatment is sequential: fluid first (saline), then anti-resorptive (bisphosphonate, denosumab if bisphosphonate-resistant or renal failure), with calcitonin for rapid temporary control. Treatment is etiological where possible. Malignancy-associated humoral hypercalcaemia (PTHrP) is the commonest severe presentation; granulomatous and vitamin D-mediated need steroids.
key_points: Saline first: 3-6 L/24 h to restore volume and calciuresis
Zoledronic acid 4 mg IV: peak effect 4-7 days, duration weeks
Calcitonin 4 IU/kg s/c 12-hourly: rapid (hours) onset but tachyphylaxis in 48 h
Denosumab: bisphosphonate-resistant or severe renal impairment
explanation.context: Mechanism-cause mapping in hypercalcaemia: PTH-mediated (primary hyperparathyroidism, lithium, tertiary hyperPTH) has raised or inappropriately normal PTH; PTHrP-mediated (squamous cell cancers, breast cancer with bone metastases) has suppressed PTH and raised PTHrP; vitamin D-mediated (granulomatous disease, lymphoma) has raised 1,25(OH)2D with normal 25-OH-D, responsive to steroid; local osteolytic (multiple myeloma, breast bone mets) has normal calciotropic hormones with high bone turnover markers. This patient's suppressed PTH with raised PTHrP and metastatic breast cancer fits humoral hypercalcaemia of malignancy.
