MASCC/ISOO guidelines for malignancy-associated hypercalcaemia

stem: A 38-year-old man with severe alcohol use disorder is admitted after 3 weeks of negligible oral intake. Body mass index 15.8 kg/m2. He has lost 9 kg over the past 2 months. Initial bloods: phosphate 0.62 mmol/L (reference 0.75-1.50), magnesium 0.58 mmol/L (0.70-1.10), potassium 3.4, sodium 138, glucose 4.2, albumin 28. The dietitian recommends commencing enteral nutrition.

data_table.Nutrition risk: BMI 15.8, 9 kg loss in 2 months, negligible oral intake 3 weeks.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step before initiating feeds?

options.text: Replace phosphate, magnesium and potassium plus give intravenous thiamine 200 mg 8-hourly, then start feeds at 5-10 kcal/kg/day
options.rationale: He is at very high risk of refeeding syndrome (BMI <16, >10% weight loss, negligible intake >10 days, alcohol misuse - any single feature is high risk; he has multiple). Pre-feed electrolyte replacement, intravenous thiamine (Wernicke prophylaxis), and slow caloric introduction (5-10 kcal/kg/day, building over a week) are NICE-endorsed practice. Phosphate, magnesium and potassium must be replaced before and during feeding because insulin-driven intracellular shift on carbohydrate load will drop them dangerously.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal - Refeeding syndrome
NICE Nutrition Support guidelines (referenced by AuSPEN)

options.text: Start full enteral feeds at 25 kcal/kg/day with daily electrolyte monitoring
options.rationale: Full-rate feeds in high-risk refeeding patient precipitate severe hypophosphataemia, hypomagnesaemia, hypokalaemia, thiamine deficiency exacerbation (Wernicke, beriberi), cardiac arrhythmia and respiratory failure. Slow start is essential (NICE; eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal - Refeeding syndrome

options.text: Start oral feeds with high-protein supplements and oral thiamine 100 mg daily
options.rationale: Oral thiamine has poor bioavailability in alcohol use disorder; intravenous loading is required to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy. Oral protein supplements deliver a high caloric load too quickly (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal - Refeeding syndrome

options.text: Start total parenteral nutrition at standard rates and monitor electrolytes daily
options.rationale: Parenteral nutrition is reserved for non-functional gut; an enteric route is preferred. TPN at standard rates carries the same refeeding risk and additional line-related and metabolic risks (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal - Refeeding syndrome

options.text: Defer feeding for 48 hours and replace electrolytes alone
options.rationale: Prolonged fasting worsens malnutrition; the right approach is replace electrolytes AND start low-rate feeds in parallel, building gradually. Total deferral is harmful (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal - Refeeding syndrome

explanation.summary: Refeeding syndrome occurs when carbohydrate delivery after prolonged starvation triggers insulin secretion, driving phosphate, magnesium and potassium intracellularly. The acutely depleted serum levels cause cardiac arrhythmia, respiratory failure (phosphate-dependent ATP synthesis), seizures and rhabdomyolysis. Prevention: identify risk, replace electrolytes, give IV thiamine before feeds, start at 5-10 kcal/kg/day, build over 7-10 days.
key_points: High-risk features (NICE): BMI <16, weight loss >15% in 3-6 months, intake <5 days, low electrolytes
IV thiamine 200-300 mg before feed (prevents Wernicke and wet beriberi)
Start 5-10 kcal/kg/day, advance by 5 kcal/kg/day if electrolytes stable
Monitor phosphate, magnesium, potassium 12-hourly for first 72 h
explanation.context: During starvation, intracellular phosphate stores deplete despite serum levels appearing normal (whole-body deficit masked by extracellular shift). Carbohydrate delivery stimulates insulin, which drives cellular uptake of glucose along with phosphate (for ATP synthesis), magnesium and potassium. The serum phosphate can drop below 0.3 mmol/L within hours, impairing oxygen delivery (2,3-DPG depletion), muscle contractility, and ATP-dependent cellular processes. Thiamine deficiency superimposed on glucose loading precipitates Wernicke encephalopathy (the classical 'glucose without thiamine in alcoholic' pitfall).

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal - Refeeding syndrome
NICE Nutrition Support guidelines (referenced by AuSPEN)

stem: Five hospitalised patients are noted to have hypoglycaemia. The endocrinology registrar is asked to identify which patient most likely has a sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycaemia requiring prolonged observation and octreotide rather than the standard short observation after glucose correction.

data_table.Patient 1: Type 1 diabetic on insulin pump, BGL 2.4 at 6am after exercise yesterday evening.
data_table.Patient 2: Type 2 diabetic on metformin and gliclazide, BGL 2.1 at 4pm, recurrent episodes overnight despite glucose.
data_table.Patient 3: Post-bariatric surgery patient, BGL 2.6 90 minutes after a high-carbohydrate meal.
data_table.Patient 4: Adrenal insufficient patient who missed hydrocortisone dose, BGL 2.8.
data_table.Patient 5: End-stage liver disease patient with BGL 2.5 fasting.

lead_in: Which patient requires prolonged observation and octreotide?

options.text: Patient 1 - exercise-related insulin pump hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: Exogenous insulin hypoglycaemia resolves within hours of cessation. Standard glucose correction and continued monitoring suffice; pump reprogramming and education prevent recurrence (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hypoglycaemia

options.text: Patient 2 - gliclazide-associated recurrent hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: Sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycaemia is characteristically prolonged and recurrent because gliclazide stimulates ongoing insulin secretion until the drug clears (half-life 12-24 hours). Standard glucose boluses are repeatedly overcome by continued insulin release. Octreotide suppresses pancreatic insulin secretion at the beta cell, breaking the cycle. Observation must extend at least 24 hours from the last dose.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hypoglycaemia
Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness - Sulfonylurea poisoning

options.text: Patient 3 - post-bariatric reactive hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: Post-bariatric (dumping) hypoglycaemia is reactive (high glucose-driven insulin overshoot 1-3 h post-meal). Managed by dietary modification (low glycaemic index, small frequent meals), not octreotide for the acute episode (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hypoglycaemia

options.text: Patient 4 - adrenal insufficiency-related hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: Adrenal hypoglycaemia resolves with hydrocortisone replacement, not octreotide. The deficit is glucocorticoid (cortisol is a counter-regulatory hormone) not endogenous insulin excess (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hypoglycaemia

options.text: Patient 5 - hepatic failure hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: Hepatic failure hypoglycaemia reflects depleted hepatic glycogen and impaired gluconeogenesis. Treated with continuous glucose infusion (10% dextrose) and addressing underlying liver disease, not octreotide (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hypoglycaemia

explanation.summary: Hypoglycaemia mechanism determines management. Sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycaemia is prolonged because the drug keeps stimulating insulin secretion until cleared (12-24 hours for gliclazide). Octreotide (somatostatin analogue) directly suppresses beta-cell insulin secretion, breaking the cycle. Other causes (exogenous insulin, post-bariatric, adrenal, hepatic) have different mechanisms and different treatments.
key_points: Sulfonylurea: 24-h observation, octreotide 50 mcg s/c 6-hourly if recurrent
Exogenous insulin: glucose, then observe; pump/regime adjust
Reactive post-bariatric: dietary, acarbose if persistent
Counter-regulatory deficit (adrenal/hepatic): replace deficient hormone or support metabolism
explanation.context: Sulfonylureas bind the SUR1 subunit of the ATP-sensitive potassium channel on the pancreatic beta cell, closing it independently of glucose levels. Insulin secretion continues until the drug is cleared. Glucose infusion temporarily raises BGL but stimulates further insulin secretion (because beta cells are pharmacologically primed), creating a vicious cycle of hypoglycaemia-glucose-hypoglycaemia. Octreotide binds somatostatin receptor 2 on the beta cell, suppressing insulin release at the source and breaking the cycle. Half-life considerations: glibenclamide >24 hours (longer observation), glipizide ~6 hours, gliclazide modified release ~24 hours.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hypoglycaemia
Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness - Sulfonylurea poisoning

stem: A 31-year-old woman presents 4 months postpartum with palpitations, anxiety, heat intolerance and weight loss of 3 kg over 6 weeks. She is breastfeeding. Examination: small diffuse goitre without bruit, no eye signs, no pretibial myxoedema, fine tremor. Heart rate 102, blood pressure 122/72. TSH <0.01 mU/L, free T4 32 pmol/L, free T3 9 pmol/L. TSH receptor antibodies negative, anti-thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) antibodies 480 IU/mL (raised). Iodine-123 uptake very low at 1.2% (reference 5-25%).

data_table.Symptoms: Palpitations, anxiety, heat intolerance, 3 kg weight loss in 6 weeks.
data_table.Examination: Small diffuse goitre (no bruit), no eye signs, fine tremor.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Propranolol 20-40 mg orally 8-hourly for symptom control without thionamide
options.rationale: Postpartum thyroiditis is a destructive thyroiditis: low uptake on iodine scan, raised anti-TPO antibodies, TRAb negative, no Graves features (eye signs, vascular goitre with bruit). Hyperthyroid phase is self-limiting (4-8 weeks), often followed by hypothyroid phase before recovery. Treatment is symptomatic with beta-blocker only; thionamides are ineffective (no new hormone synthesis) and inappropriate.
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum position
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Thyrotoxicosis

options.text: Carbimazole 10 mg orally 8-hourly with regular thyroid function monitoring
options.rationale: Carbimazole blocks new hormone synthesis but postpartum thyroiditis releases preformed hormone from a destructive process. Thionamide is ineffective and unnecessary; would also pass into breast milk (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum position

options.text: Radioactive iodine ablation as definitive therapy
options.rationale: Radioactive iodine is contraindicated during breastfeeding (concentrates in milk). Also unnecessary in self-limiting thyroiditis where uptake is already very low (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum position

options.text: Total thyroidectomy because of suspected Graves disease
options.rationale: TRAb negative, low uptake, no Graves-specific features (orbitopathy, pretibial myxoedema, vascular goitre with bruit) make Graves unlikely. Surgery is not indicated (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum position

options.text: Levothyroxine 50 mcg daily for the hyperthyroid phase
options.rationale: Levothyroxine would worsen current hyperthyroidism. It is reserved for the subsequent hypothyroid phase if symptomatic or persistent beyond 6 months (Endocrine Society of Australia).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum position

explanation.summary: Postpartum thyroiditis and Graves disease are distinguished by uptake scan (low in thyroiditis, high in Graves), antibodies (anti-TPO in thyroiditis, TRAb in Graves) and clinical features (Graves-specific eye signs/pretibial myxoedema/vascular goitre in Graves). Thyroiditis is self-limiting and managed with beta-blocker; Graves needs definitive treatment (thionamide, radioiodine or surgery).
key_points: Postpartum thyroiditis: low uptake, anti-TPO raised, hyper to hypo to recovery course
Graves: high uptake, TRAb positive, eye signs/pretibial myxoedema possible
Beta-blocker compatible with breastfeeding (propranolol preferred)
Up to 50% of postpartum thyroiditis progress to permanent hypothyroidism over years
explanation.context: Postpartum thyroiditis is autoimmune thyroid disease with a postpartum trigger (likely immune rebound after pregnancy's relative immunosuppression). The destructive phase releases preformed thyroid hormone from damaged follicles, mimicking hyperthyroidism without increased synthesis (hence low radioactive iodine uptake). After the stored hormone is depleted, transient hypothyroidism occurs (4-8 weeks) before most patients recover. The uptake scan distinction matters therapeutically: thionamides work only when synthesis is upregulated (Graves), whereas symptom control with beta-blocker is sufficient for destructive thyroiditis.

sources.label: Endocrine Society of Australia thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum position statement
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Thyrotoxicosis

stem: A 42-year-old man 5 days after pituitary macroadenoma resection develops polyuria (8 L in 24 hours) and increasing thirst. Urine output is consistent over time. Plasma sodium has risen from 138 to 152 mmol/L over the same 24 hours. Plasma osmolality 312 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 122 mOsm/kg, urine sodium 28 mmol/L. He is conscious, drinking water voluntarily, but is unable to keep up with urine losses.

data_table.Osmolality: Plasma 312 mOsm/kg, urine 122 mOsm/kg, urine Na 28.
data_table.Hydration: Conscious, drinking, but losing more than able to replace.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate diagnosis and immediate management?

options.text: Central diabetes insipidus; commence desmopressin 1-2 mcg subcutaneously and free water replacement
options.rationale: Post-pituitary surgery polyuria with hypotonic urine (urine osm <300 with plasma osm >295), rising sodium, and consistent timing reflects central diabetes insipidus from disruption of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) production in posterior pituitary or stalk. Desmopressin (synthetic ADH analogue) corrects the deficit; free water replacement addresses established hypernatraemia (cap correction at 10 mmol/L per 24 h to prevent cerebral oedema from rapid water shift).
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia diabetes insipidus position
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Diabetes insipidus

options.text: SIADH; fluid restrict to 800 mL/day and consider tolvaptan
options.rationale: SIADH would produce hyponatraemia with concentrated urine (urine osm >100 in setting of low plasma osm). This patient has hypernatraemia with dilute urine, the opposite picture (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyponatraemia

options.text: Cerebral salt wasting; replace volume with 0.9% saline at 200 mL/h
options.rationale: Cerebral salt wasting causes hyponatraemia with hypovolaemia and high urine sodium (>40), typically after SAH or neurosurgery. This patient has hypernatraemia (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyponatraemia

options.text: Osmotic diuresis from hyperglycaemia; check BGL and start insulin
options.rationale: Osmotic diuresis from hyperglycaemia would show high urine osmolality (from glucose) and serum hyperglycaemia. Urine osm here is low (122) suggesting water diuresis, not solute diuresis (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Diabetes insipidus

options.text: Primary polydipsia; restrict free water to 1.5 L per day
options.rationale: Primary polydipsia would produce hyponatraemia and dilute urine, with normal ADH response. This patient has hypernatraemia despite high intake, indicating he cannot keep up with renal water losses (eTG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Diabetes insipidus

explanation.summary: Polyuria with hypotonic urine and rising sodium after pituitary surgery is central diabetes insipidus. Distinguish from nephrogenic DI (renal ADH resistance) by desmopressin response: central DI corrects, nephrogenic does not. Treat central DI with desmopressin (s/c, IV, intranasal or oral) titrated to urine output and sodium, alongside careful free water replacement (cap correction at 10 mmol/L per 24 h).
key_points: Central DI: ADH deficit; desmopressin corrects
Nephrogenic DI: ADH resistance; treat cause (lithium, hypercalcaemia)
Cap sodium correction at 10 mmol/L per 24 h (cerebral oedema risk)
Post-pituitary triphasic response: DI, then SIADH, then permanent DI
explanation.context: Pituitary surgery may produce a triphasic response: initial DI from immediate stalk injury (days 1-5), transient SIADH from release of stored hormone (days 5-10), and permanent DI in some (after 10 days). The discrimination matrix uses three variables: plasma sodium (hyper in DI, hypo in SIADH), urine osmolality (low in DI, high in SIADH) and ADH response (intact in primary polydipsia, deficient in central DI, present but ineffective in nephrogenic DI). Diabetes insipidus in this acute postoperative setting requires close monitoring because conscious patients can usually keep up with losses if water is available, but obtunded or post-anaesthetic patients lose the thirst-drive safety net and develop dangerous hypernatraemia quickly.

sources.label: Endocrine Society of Australia diabetes insipidus position statement
Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Diabetes insipidus
